original_abstract: Electrochemical ammonia synthesis via the nitrate reduction reaction (NO3RR) has emerged as a compelling alternative to the carbon-intensive Haber-Bosch process. However, while most current research targets the low nitrate concentrations found in wastewater, the high-concentration regimes typical of industrial fertilizer and nuclear waste remain largely unaddressed. Achieving high-rate production in these concentrated electrolytes (≥1 M) is traditionally limited by sluggish hydrogen transfer kinetics. Here, we present a synergistic Ru/Cu₂O cocatalytic system capable of driving NO3RR at a high current of 10.0 A within a 16 cm² flow electrolyzer, achieving 100% Faradaic efficiency toward ammonia in 1 M nitrate. Through deuterium labeling and *operando* Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, we characterize the intermediate species and quantify the accelerated hydrogen transfer rates on the Ru/Cu₂O surface. Furthermore, *ab initio* molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations reveal that adsorbed hydroxide on the Ru nanoparticles reorganizes the interfacial hydrogen-bonded water network, increasing its density near the Cu₂O surface to facilitate rapid proton relay. This work underscores the critical role of engineering multimetallic interfaces to overcome kinetic bottlenecks in high-rate electrosynthesis.
humanized_abstract: Ammonia synthesis via the nitrate reduction reaction (NO3RR) has the potential to become a more efficient process for producing ammonia than the widely used Haber-Bosch process while at the same time reducing greenhouse gas emissions. While a large body of work has explored the mechanisms involved with low nitrate concentrations of wastewater origin, there has been very limited work on the high concentration regime that is involved with industrial fertilizer and nuclear waste production streams. Operation of the NO3RR at the required high productivity rates in these high-concentration electrolytes (≥1 M) is limited due to hindered hydrogen transport. A synergistic Ru/Cu₂O cocatalyst was developed here for production of ammonia at a current of 10.0 A within a 16 cm² flow electrolyzer while still achieving 100% Faradaic efficiency for the target product in a 1 M solution of nitrate. An in-depth analysis of intermediate species present at the catalyst surface was accomplished via several techniques such as deuterium labeling and *operando* Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) while elucidating the role of the cocatalyst at high currents and electrolyte concentrations. Additional analysis from *ab initio* molecular dynamics (AIMD) revealed that the adsorbed Ru hydroxide affected the interfacial hydrogen-bonded structure of water by increasing the density near the Cu₂O surface, while facilitating proton transport. Hence, this study demonstrates the impact of engineered multimetallic catalyst surfaces on optimizing the ammonia synthesis rate for improving electrosynthesis productivity.
